definition: Meta-finding that demographic direction is near-certain but magnitude and timing predictions are systematically overconfident. The split between directional confidence (7-9/10) and magnitude confidence (3-6/10) is the single most important methodological finding of the analysis.
evidence: 14/14 precise quantitative claims examined either had confidence intervals too wide for decisions or rested on fragile identification
Every debate pair independently converged on the direction vs. magnitude split
Historical base rate of demographic predictions being wrong on magnitude is robust
The consensus converts directional certainty into magnitude certainty with insufficient evidence
open_questions: Is there a systematic way to improve magnitude calibration for demographic-financial predictions?
Which specific demographic-financial channels have the narrowest confidence intervals?
